key_insight: Strain rate and indentation length scale affect nanoindentation differently: strain rate sets the thermally activated flow stress, while indentation depth controls plastic-zone growth, pile-up, and GND buildup.
engineering_relevance: A calibrated strain-gradient crystal plasticity model can predict irradiated tungsten response across strain rates and length scales, supporting constitutive design for transient and radiation-damaged components.
method: Spherical nanoindentation on unirradiated and neutron-irradiated single-crystal tungsten over 3.2e-5 to 3.2e-3 s^-1.
AFM and HR-EBSD to measure pile-up, residual lattice strain, and GND distributions.
Strain-gradient CPFE with thermally activated slip, GND hardening, irradiation obstacle hardening, and strain-dependent softening; calibrated once and validated across conditions.
evidence_signal: The model was fit to a single experimental condition and then validated against all other strain rates, while independently varying strain rate and indentation depth in simulation.
watch_item: The constitutive law is calibrated for this specific material/state, so transfer to other irradiation levels, temperatures, or microstructures may require re-parameterization.
paper_title: Decoupling Strain-Rate Sensitivity and Deformation Length Scale Effects in Neutron-Irradiated Tungsten: A Coupled Nano-Indentation, HR-EBSD and Crystal Plasticity Study
